questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Community Fridge
The first community fridge on Linden Street began with an old refrigerator, a painted wooden shelter, and a sign that read, “Take what you need. Leave what you can.” It stood outside a neighborhood center where residents could place unopened packaged foods, fresh produce, and bottled drinks for anyone to take.
At first, some people doubted that the project would last. They worried the fridge would become messy, empty, or unsafe. The neighborhood center responded by creating clear rules. Volunteers checked the fridge twice a day, removed expired items, cleaned the shelves, and recorded the temperature. A list on the side of the shelter explained what could be donated and what could not. Homemade meals, opened containers, and raw meat were not accepted.
Within a few months, the fridge became more than a place to leave extra food. A local bakery began donating unsold bread twice a week. Gardeners brought tomatoes, peppers, and herbs during the summer. Families who did not qualify for some assistance programs used the fridge when paychecks ran short. People also began leaving notes: “Thank you for the apples,” or “More baby food would help.”
The fridge did not end food insecurity in the neighborhood. It could not replace a stable income, affordable groceries, or larger food programs. Still, it created a small, reliable way for neighbors to share resources directly. Its success depended not only on generosity, but also on organization.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A community fridge can help neighbors share food when generosity is supported by clear rules and regular care.
choices.B: A community fridge is useful only when it accepts homemade meals and raw meat.
choices.C: The Linden Street project failed because residents did not donate enough food.
choices.D: Food insecurity can be solved completely by placing one refrigerator outside.

questions.correctAnswerText: A community fridge can help neighbors share food when generosity is supported by clear rules and regular care.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that the fridge helped residents, but its success depended on rules, cleaning, monitoring, and organization.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Community Fridge.”
Why did the neighborhood center create clear rules for the fridge?

choices.A: To make sure only bakery owners could use the fridge
choices.B: To address concerns about mess, safety, and reliability
choices.C: To prevent gardeners from donating fresh produce
choices.D: To replace larger food programs in the city

questions.correctAnswerText: To address concerns about mess, safety, and reliability
questions.explanation: The rules were created after people worried the fridge might become messy, empty, or unsafe.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: Volunteers checked the fridge twice a day.
choices.B: The center accepted all homemade meals.
choices.C: Volunteers removed expired items.
choices.D: The fridge was locked except during business hours.
choices.E: Residents were required to pay before taking food.

questions.correctAnswerText: Volunteers checked the fridge twice a day. | Volunteers removed expired items.
questions.explanation: The passage lists both actions as part of the project’s safety and organization routine.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Community Fridge.”
What can be inferred from the notes people left near the fridge?

choices.A: The fridge was being used by people with real needs and preferences.
choices.B: Residents wanted the fridge removed immediately.
choices.C: The bakery stopped donating bread after the first week.
choices.D: The fridge accepted only apples and baby food.

questions.correctAnswerText: The fridge was being used by people with real needs and preferences.
questions.explanation: Notes of thanks and requests for baby food show that residents used the fridge for specific needs.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Community Fridge.”
In the sentence “Its success depended not only on generosity, but also on organization,” the word generosity most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=willingness to give or help
questions.explanation: In context, generosity means people’s willingness to donate food or help neighbors.

tiles.C: The fridge could not replace stable income or larger food programs.
tiles.D: The fridge accepted opened containers and raw meat.

questions.prompt:
Read the community notice and answer the question.
Tenant Repair Request Update
To: Residents of Riverbend Apartments
From: Property Management Office
Subject: New Repair Request System
Beginning August 5, all non-emergency repair requests must be submitted through the resident portal or by calling the office during business hours. The new system will assign each request a tracking number so residents can check its status.
Non-emergency repairs include dripping faucets, loose cabinet handles, stuck closet doors, broken window screens, and appliance problems that do not create an immediate safety risk. Emergency repairs should still be reported by calling the 24-hour maintenance line. Emergencies include active flooding, no heat during cold weather, electrical sparks, broken exterior locks, or any condition that makes an apartment unsafe.
When submitting a request, include the apartment number, a brief description of the problem, and the best times for maintenance staff to enter. If possible, attach a photo. Clear descriptions help staff bring the correct tools and parts on the first visit.
Residents who have already submitted a request do not need to submit it again. Requests made before August 5 will be entered into the new system by office staff. Residents without internet access may call the office or visit the lobby kiosk during regular office hours.
What is the main purpose of the notice?

choices.A: To explain how residents should submit and track non-emergency repair requests
choices.B: To announce that all repair requests have been canceled
choices.C: To require residents to complete their own apartment repairs
choices.D: To tell residents that emergency repairs must be submitted only through the portal

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how residents should submit and track non-emergency repair requests
questions.explanation: The notice explains the new repair request system, including tracking numbers and submission methods.

Refer to “Tenant Repair Request Update.”

choices.A: A loose cabinet handle
choices.B: A stuck closet door
choices.C: Electrical sparks
choices.D: A broken window screen

questions.correctAnswerText: Electrical sparks
questions.explanation: The notice lists electrical sparks as an emergency that should be reported through the 24-hour maintenance line.

choices.D: A request to cancel all previous maintenance visits

questions.correctAnswerText: The apartment number | A brief description of the problem
questions.explanation: The notice asks residents to include the apartment number, a problem description, and best entry times.

choices.A: Photos help staff bring the correct tools and parts on the first visit.

choices.C: Photos replace the need to describe the problem.
choices.D: Photos allow residents to avoid giving an apartment number.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Make Room for Walking Meetings
Businesses should encourage walking meetings for small groups when the topic does not require a screen or confidential documents. A walking meeting is exactly what it sounds like: two or three coworkers discuss a work issue while walking indoors or outdoors. It is not appropriate for every situation, but it can be a useful option.
Walking meetings can improve focus. Many employees spend most of the day sitting at desks, moving from one screen-based task to another. A short walk can help people think through a problem without the usual distractions of email alerts and office interruptions. Some supervisors report that walking meetings are especially helpful for brainstorming, project check-ins, and mentoring conversations.
They can also support health without turning the workplace into a gym. A fifteen-minute walk around the building will not replace regular exercise, but it does add movement to a long workday. Employees who feel uncomfortable with intense wellness programs may be more willing to take part in a simple walking conversation.
Critics may argue that walking meetings are inefficient or exclude people with mobility needs. Those concerns matter. Walking meetings should be optional, short, and limited to topics that can be discussed safely. Teams should choose accessible routes or offer seated alternatives. With these guidelines, walking meetings can become one more flexible tool for communication, not a required workplace trend.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Businesses should use walking meetings as an optional communication tool for suitable situations.
choices.B: Businesses should replace all meetings with outdoor exercise.
choices.C: Employees should discuss confidential documents while walking through public spaces.
choices.D: Walking meetings should be required for every employee every day.

questions.correctAnswerText: Businesses should use walking meetings as an optional communication tool for suitable situations.
questions.explanation: The author supports walking meetings only for appropriate topics and with accessibility guidelines.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Make Room for Walking Meetings.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that walking meetings can improve focus?

choices.A: Some supervisors report that walking meetings help with brainstorming and project check-ins.
choices.B: Walking meetings are not appropriate for every situation.
choices.C: Employees may feel uncomfortable with intense wellness programs.
choices.D: Teams should offer seated alternatives.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some supervisors report that walking meetings help with brainstorming and project check-ins.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports the idea that walking meetings can improve focus and communication.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: Walking meetings may not work for topics requiring screens or confidential documents.
choices.B: Walking meetings automatically solve every workplace health problem.
choices.C: Walking meetings may exclude people with mobility needs if not planned carefully.
choices.D: Employees never need seated meetings.

questions.correctAnswerText: Walking meetings may not work for topics requiring screens or confidential documents. | Walking meetings may exclude people with mobility needs if not planned carefully.
questions.explanation: The author explicitly acknowledges both limitations.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Make Room for Walking Meetings.”
How does the author respond to concerns about accessibility?

choices.A: By saying that people with mobility needs should not attend meetings
choices.B: By recommending accessible routes and seated alternatives
choices.C: By arguing that accessibility does not matter for short meetings
choices.D: By suggesting that walking meetings be held only on stairs

questions.correctAnswerText: By recommending accessible routes and seated alternatives
questions.explanation: The author responds to accessibility concerns by proposing inclusive guidelines.

The office hallway has gray carpet and several framed photographs.

questions.correct: D1=In a pilot program at one office, teams reported that short walking check-ins reduced repeated follow-up emails.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In a pilot program at one office, teams reported that short walking check-ins reduced repeated follow-up emails.
questions.explanation: This sentence provides relevant evidence that walking meetings can improve workplace communication.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Make Room for Walking Meetings.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Some work conversations can be productive without screens, documents, or a conference room.
choices.B: Every employee has the same physical ability and schedule.
choices.C: Long meetings are always better than short meetings.
choices.D: Email alerts are the only problem in modern workplaces.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some work conversations can be productive without screens, documents, or a conference room.
questions.explanation: The author’s argument depends on the idea that certain discussions can happen effectively while walking.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Missing Page
Leah had repaired hundreds of books at the small-town library, but the old atlas made her pause. It was heavy, with cloth corners worn smooth by years of hands. A note inside the cover said it had belonged to a family that ran a travel agency before the highway bypassed Main Street.
Most pages needed only gentle cleaning. Then Leah found a torn edge between the maps of South America and Africa. One page was missing. The empty space bothered her more than the cracked spine. A missing page made the atlas feel unfinished, as if someone had taken a doorway out of a house.
She searched the library catalog, then the storage closet, then a box of donated maps waiting to be sorted. Nothing matched. At lunch, she mentioned the atlas to Mr. Chen, who visited the library every Wednesday to read newspapers. He smiled and said his sister had worked at the travel agency as a teenager. “She kept scraps from everything,” he said. “Postcards, ticket stubs, maps. I’ll ask.”
Two days later, Mr. Chen returned with a folded atlas page wrapped in tissue paper. The colors had faded, but the paper size and print matched. Leah did not ask how the page had left the book years before. She only smoothed it gently and began the repair.
When the atlas returned to the reference shelf, most visitors would not notice the mended seam. Leah noticed. To her, the atlas no longer looked perfect, but it looked whole.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Leah must decide whether to close the library early.
choices.B: Leah tries to restore an old atlas after discovering that a page is missing.
choices.C: Mr. Chen wants to sell newspapers at the library.
choices.D: A travel agency asks Leah to remove maps from the reference shelf.

questions.correctAnswerText: Leah tries to restore an old atlas after discovering that a page is missing.
questions.explanation: The excerpt centers on Leah’s effort to repair the atlas and make it whole again.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Missing Page.”
Why does the missing page bother Leah more than the cracked spine?

choices.A: A cracked spine cannot be repaired by anyone.
choices.B: The missing page makes the atlas feel incomplete.
choices.C: The page contains a map Leah dislikes.
choices.D: Leah wants to replace the atlas with a newspaper.

questions.correctAnswerText: The missing page makes the atlas feel incomplete.
questions.explanation: Leah compares the missing page to a doorway taken out of a house, showing that it makes the atlas feel unfinished.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Missing Page.”

choices.A: He provides a connection to the atlas’s past and helps Leah find the missing page.
choices.B: He prevents Leah from repairing the atlas.
choices.C: He asks the library to throw away all old books.
choices.D: He teaches Leah how to run a travel agency.

questions.correctAnswerText: He provides a connection to the atlas’s past and helps Leah find the missing page.
questions.explanation: Mr. Chen knows someone connected to the travel agency and returns with the missing page.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: She searches the catalog, storage closet, and donated maps.
choices.B: She ignores the torn edge between two maps.
choices.C: She smooths the returned page gently before beginning the repair.
choices.D: She asks Mr. Chen to stop visiting the library.
choices.E: She removes the atlas permanently from the library.

questions.correctAnswerText: She searches the catalog, storage closet, and donated maps. | She smooths the returned page gently before beginning the repair.
questions.explanation: These details show Leah’s careful effort to restore the atlas.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Missing Page.”

choices.A: has regained a sense of completeness despite showing signs of age.
choices.B: is now worth more money than every other book in the library.
choices.C: no longer contains any evidence of repair.
choices.D: should be kept away from all library visitors.

questions.correctAnswerText: has regained a sense of completeness despite showing signs of age.
questions.explanation: The atlas is not perfect, but the repair makes it feel whole again.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Missing Page.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Leah finds a torn edge where one page is missing.
tiles.B: Mr. Chen remembers that his sister saved materials from the travel agency.
tiles.C: Leah repairs the page and returns the atlas to the reference shelf.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
The Label on the Package
Many people have thrown away food because of a date printed on the package. However, food date labels can be confusing because they often describe quality rather than safety. A “best if used by” date usually means the manufacturer expects the food to taste freshest before that date. It does not always mean the food becomes unsafe the next day.
A “use by” date may be more important for certain refrigerated foods, especially items that spoil quickly. Even then, the date should be considered along with signs such as smell, texture, damaged packaging, or whether the food has been stored at a safe temperature. A carton of milk left in a hot car for hours may spoil before the date. A sealed box of dry pasta may remain usable long after its quality date.
Understanding labels can reduce waste and save money. Families may throw away unopened foods that are still usable simply because the printed date has passed. Stores and food pantries also face decisions about what can be donated or distributed. Clearer labeling and better consumer education can help people make safer and less wasteful choices.
The passage does not suggest ignoring food safety. Instead, it argues that people should understand what labels mean and use them with common sense. Dates are useful information, but they are not the only information.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Food date labels should be understood as one piece of information when judging quality and safety.
choices.B: All food is unsafe after the date printed on the package.
choices.C: Dry pasta spoils faster than refrigerated milk.
choices.D: People should ignore all package labels when buying food.

questions.correctAnswerText: Food date labels should be understood as one piece of information when judging quality and safety.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that printed dates are useful but should be considered along with other signs and storage conditions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Label on the Package.”
What does a “best if used by” date usually indicate?

choices.A: The date after which the food must legally be thrown away
choices.B: The period when the manufacturer expects the food to taste freshest
choices.C: The exact day the food was packaged
choices.D: The temperature at which the food was stored

questions.correctAnswerText: The period when the manufacturer expects the food to taste freshest
questions.explanation: The passage says “best if used by” usually refers to quality or freshness.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Label on the Package.”
Which example best supports the idea that storage conditions matter?

choices.A: A sealed box of dry pasta may remain usable after its quality date.
choices.B: A carton of milk left in a hot car may spoil before the printed date.
choices.C: Families may throw away unopened foods.
choices.D: Food pantries face decisions about donations.

questions.correctAnswerText: A carton of milk left in a hot car may spoil before the printed date.
questions.explanation: This example shows that storage conditions affect safety and quality.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Label on the Package.”
What can be inferred about the author’s attitude toward food safety?

choices.A: The author thinks safety should be considered carefully, not ignored.
choices.B: The author believes printed dates are never useful.
choices.C: The author wants people to eat spoiled food to save money.
choices.D: The author argues that refrigerated foods cannot spoil.

questions.correctAnswerText: The author thinks safety should be considered carefully, not ignored.
questions.explanation: The passage says it does not suggest ignoring food safety and recommends common sense.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Label on the Package.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It explains confusion about labels, gives examples, and recommends informed judgment.
choices.B: It tells a story about a single family shopping for groceries.
choices.C: It lists food companies in order of size.
choices.D: It argues that stores should stop selling refrigerated food.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains confusion about labels, gives examples, and recommends informed judgment.
questions.explanation: The passage defines label confusion, provides food examples, and advises using labels with common sense.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Bike Light Giveaway
(1) The Eastside Safety Coalition will give away free bike lights at the transit plaza next Friday. (2) Volunteers will install front and rear lights on bicycles while supplies last. (3) The event is intended for riders who travel before sunrise or after sunset. (4) [D1], the coalition will hand out reflective ankle bands and safety cards. (5) Each rider [D2] asked to sign a simple form confirming that the lights were received. (6) The group hopes the event will help drivers see cyclists more clearly during darker hours.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In addition; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “In addition” adds another giveaway item, and “will be asked” is the correct verb phrase.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Bike Light Giveaway.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: The group hopes the event will help drivers see cyclists more clearly during darker hours.
choices.B: The group is hopeful that the event will be something that helps drivers see cyclists in a clearer way when hours are darker.
choices.C: The group hopes, with hope, that cyclists will be seen by drivers during hours that are dark.
choices.D: The event is hoped by the group to help the seeing of cyclists by drivers more clearly.

questions.correctAnswerText: The group hopes the event will help drivers see cyclists more clearly during darker hours.
questions.explanation: This version is concise and keeps the original meaning.

choices.D: The list of safety tips are posted on the sign.

questions.correctAnswerText: The volunteers who install the lights arrive at noon.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “volunteers” correctly takes the plural verb “arrive.”

choices.B: Elena called Marisol after the meeting, and Marisol was relieved.
choices.C: She was relieved when Elena called Marisol after the meeting.
choices.D: When the meeting ended, she called her and felt relieved.

questions.correctAnswerText: Elena called Marisol after the meeting, and Marisol was relieved.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Marisol” makes clear who was relieved.

The neighborhood clinic added evening flu-shot appointments. [D1], more working adults were able to schedule visits.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.B: While the copies were printing, the receptionist answered the phone.

choices.D: While the copies, were printing the receptionist answered the phone.

questions.prompt:

The restaurant posted a notice. The notice explained the new pickup window.

choices.B: The notice posted the restaurant and explained the new pickup window.

choices.A: The coordinator checked the list and adds three names yesterday.
choices.B: The coordinator checks the list and added three names yesterday.
choices.C: The coordinator checked the list and added three names yesterday.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Keep the Community Bulletin Board
The library should keep its physical community bulletin board near the front entrance. Some people argue that announcements should move entirely online, but the bulletin board serves residents who do not check social media or who have limited internet access. Job fairs, free health screenings, neighborhood meetings, and lost-pet notices are easy to see when people enter the building.
A physical board also creates a shared public space. When announcements are posted together, residents can discover events they were not specifically searching for. Someone visiting for a book may notice a tenant-rights workshop or a free concert in the park. Online platforms often show people only what they already follow.
The board does need better management. Old flyers should be removed weekly, and postings should include dates, contact information, and clear language. These improvements would make the bulletin board more useful without eliminating it. The library should update the board, not replace it with an online-only system.
Passage B: Move Announcements Online
The library should replace the crowded bulletin board with an online community calendar. The current board is often messy, outdated, and difficult to read. Flyers overlap, important information is hidden, and staff spend time answering questions about events that have already passed.
An online calendar would be easier to search and update. Residents could filter events by date, topic, or location. Community groups could submit announcements through a simple form, and staff could approve them before they appear. This system would reduce clutter and make information more accurate.
Supporters of the physical board point out that not everyone has easy internet access. That concern is real, but the library already offers public computers and staff help. The library could also print a weekly one-page calendar for visitors who prefer paper. A modern system can still include offline support while avoiding the confusion of an overcrowded wall.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A favors keeping a managed physical board, while Passage B favors replacing it with an online calendar plus paper support.
choices.B: Passage A argues that libraries should stop posting announcements, while Passage B argues that libraries should close public computers.
choices.C: Passage A supports paid advertising, while Passage B supports banning community groups.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on lost books, while Passage B focuses on library fines.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A favors keeping a managed physical board, while Passage B favors replacing it with an online calendar plus paper support.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about whether to keep the physical board or move to a mostly online system.

questions.prompt:

choices.B: An online calendar could be filtered by date, topic, or location.
choices.C: Staff could approve submissions before they appear online.
choices.D: The current board is sometimes crowded and outdated.

questions.correctAnswerText: The bulletin board helps residents who do not check social media or have limited internet access.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s argument for keeping the physical board.

choices.E: Community groups should be prevented from sharing announcements.

questions.correctAnswerText: An online calendar would allow residents to search events by date, topic, or location. | A weekly printed calendar could help visitors who prefer paper.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports these claims as benefits of an online system with offline support.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about library announcements.

choices.A: “The library should replace the crowded bulletin board with an online community calendar.”
choices.B: “Residents could filter events by date, topic, or location.”
choices.C: “Supporters of the physical board point out that not everyone has easy internet access.”
choices.D: “Community groups could submit announcements through a simple form.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Supporters of the physical board point out that not everyone has easy internet access.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly acknowledges Passage A’s accessibility concern.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about library announcements.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: The library needs a way to share community information that is accurate and accessible.
choices.B: The library has decided to stop sharing community information.
choices.C: All residents prefer social media to printed notices.
choices.D: The bulletin board is used only by library staff.

questions.correctAnswerText: The library needs a way to share community information that is accurate and accessible.
questions.explanation: Both passages focus on how the library can best share community announcements.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about library announcements.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data on how residents currently learn about events and how often the bulletin board is used
choices.B: A list of the library’s oldest books
choices.C: A survey about favorite fiction genres
choices.D: A map of streets far outside the library’s service area

questions.correctAnswerText: Data on how residents currently learn about events and how often the bulletin board is used
questions.explanation: This evidence would help compare the usefulness of the physical board and an online calendar.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Wrapped in brown paper, Denise carried the package to the post office.

choices.A: Wrapped in brown paper, Denise carried the package to the post office.
choices.B: Denise carried the package wrapped in brown paper to the post office.
choices.C: Denise, wrapped in brown paper, carried the package to the post office.
choices.D: The post office carried Denise’s package wrapped in brown paper.

questions.correctAnswerText: Denise carried the package wrapped in brown paper to the post office.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “package,” the thing wrapped in brown paper.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The team tested the alarm. The alarm sounded clearly in every hallway.

choices.A: The team tested the alarm, which sounded clearly in every hallway.
choices.B: The alarm tested the team and sounded every hallway clearly.
choices.C: The team tested, and every hallway sounded the alarm clearly.
choices.D: Sounding the team clearly, the hallway tested the alarm.

questions.correctAnswerText: The team tested the alarm, which sounded clearly in every hallway.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas and shows that the alarm sounded clearly.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses commas correctly?

choices.A: The garden tour, which begins at ten, will include three historic homes.
choices.B: The garden tour which begins, at ten will include three historic homes.
choices.C: The garden tour, which begins at ten will include, three historic homes.
choices.D: The garden tour which, begins at ten, will include three historic homes.

questions.correctAnswerText: The garden tour, which begins at ten, will include three historic homes.
questions.explanation: The nonessential clause “which begins at ten” is correctly set off by commas.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Ban Single-Use Plastic Bags at Checkout
The city should ban single-use plastic bags at grocery and retail checkout counters. These bags are used for only a short time but can remain in the environment for years. They blow out of trash bins, clog storm drains, and break into smaller pieces that are difficult to remove from soil and waterways. A citywide ban would encourage shoppers to bring reusable bags and would reduce one common source of litter.
The change would be manageable. Many stores already sell reusable bags near the entrance, and several nearby towns have adopted similar rules. After the first few weeks, most shoppers adjust by keeping bags in a car, backpack, or near the front door. The city could also require stores to provide recycled-paper bags for a small fee and distribute free reusable bags at libraries and community centers during the transition.
Opponents say a ban would inconvenience shoppers, especially people who forget bags or make unplanned purchases. That concern is real, but convenience should not outweigh the long-term cost of avoidable waste. The city already asks residents to sort recycling and follow trash rules because individual habits affect the whole community. Reducing plastic bag waste is another reasonable step.
Passage B:
Do Not Ban Plastic Bags
The city should not ban single-use plastic bags at checkout. Reducing waste is a worthy goal, but a ban is a blunt solution that may create new problems. Some shoppers reuse plastic bags for trash liners, pet waste, or carrying wet items. If checkout bags disappear, many residents may buy small plastic bags instead, reducing the environmental benefit.
A ban may also place a burden on people with limited income or limited transportation. Reusable bags cost money, and they must be cleaned to avoid carrying food residue. People who shop by bus or on foot may make unplanned stops and may not always have bags with them. A fee for paper bags may seem small, but it affects families who count every dollar.
The city can reduce plastic waste without a ban. Stores could offer discounts for reusable bags, place bag-recycling bins near entrances, and train cashiers not to double-bag items unless requested. Public education can encourage better habits while preserving flexibility. A policy should reduce waste without punishing shoppers for ordinary mistakes or needs.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether the city should ban single-use plastic bags at checkout? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
questions.correctAnswerText: Not auto-scored
questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
